title-group.article-title: Potential Effects of Aspirin on Transcription Factors Related to the Inflammation in Atherosclerosis: A Systematic Review

sec.title: Background and Aims
sec.p: Atherosclerosis (AS), a major cause of cardiovascular mortality, involves chronic inflammation mediated by transcription factors such as NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α. Beyond its antiplatelet role, aspirin exerts anti‐inflammatory effects that may influence these molecular pathways. This review systematically evaluates aspirin's modulatory effects on these transcription factors in AS.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Relevant English‐language studies (1980–2025) were retrieved from PubMed, ScienceDirect, and Google Scholar using keywords related to aspirin, atherosclerosis, and inflammation‐related transcription factors.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Aspirin inhibits NF‐κB activation, reduces proinflammatory cytokine production, and enhances PPAR and AP‐2α signaling, contributing to anti‐inflammatory and plaque‐stabilizing effects. Clinical findings (e.g., ARRIVE, ASPREE) show variable cardiovascular outcomes, likely reflecting dose‐ and population‐dependent responses.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Aspirin modulates key inflammatory transcription factors in AS, offering potential benefits beyond platelet inhibition. Further studies should clarify AP‐2α's mechanistic role and explore aspirin‐based combination strategies tailored to patient‐specific inflammatory profiles.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Atherosclerosis (AS), an inflammatory and chronic vascular disease, is one of the most common causes of cardiovascular disease (CVD) that is a leading mortality factor worldwide. Several key mechanisms contribute to the development of atherosclerotic plaques, including: (i) activation of dysfunctional endothelial cells and subsequent cholesterol accumulation in the vessel wall, (ii) oxidative stress caused by excessive production of free radicals, and (iii) an imbalance between pro‐inflammatory and anti‐inflammatory responses. They considered essential for regulating the expression of inflammatory genes (inflammatory modulators) associated with NF‐κB [1, 2, 3]. Several studies illustrated that during the AS process, regulation of membrane protein expression is controlled by various transcription factors such as peroxisome proliferator‐activated receptors (PPARs) in addition to NF‐κB [4, 5, 6].

Given the role of inflammation in developing atherosclerotic plaques, several treatments have been tested that can specifically act on inflammatory signals and influence inflammatory pathways at different stages of the atherosclerotic process [7, 8].

Notably, aspirin's beneficial effects on AS are not due to its antiplatelet function because platelet aggregation has little effect on the progression of AS. Besides, the plasma half‐life of aspirin is short and is hydrolyzed in human serum by the enzyme paraoxonase 1 (PON1) [9]. Acetylsalicylic acid, under the brand name aspirin is a nonsteroidal anti‐inflammatory drug that inhibits inflammation by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX), and making a suppressing effect on the generation of proinflammatory molecules like prostaglandins [10]. In hyperlipidemic conditions, aspirin systemically normalize atherosclerosis both in vitro and in vivo. It leads to a hemostatic balance between thromboxane and prostacyclin. Moreover, low‐dose aspirin treatment decreases the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [11, 12].

It is worth to mention that salicylic acid is considered to possibly affect AS by regulating transcription factors. Long‐term treatment with free salicylic acid regulates the function of transcription factors associated with the inflammatory and anti‐inflammatory mechanism, thereby controlling AS complications [13].

Despite extensive research on aspirin's cardiovascular effects, its transcriptional regulatory role in atherosclerosis has not been systematically defined. Therefore, the present review aims to address the central question: How does aspirin modulate inflammation in atherosclerosis through its effects on the key transcription factors NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α? We hypothesize that aspirin exerts pleiotropic, anti‐inflammatory effects by regulating these transcription factors in a dose‐ and context‐dependent manner. This systematic review synthesizes current molecular and clinical evidence to clarify these mechanisms and highlight their therapeutic implications.

sec.title: Literature Search
sec.p: This systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines to evaluate the effects of aspirin on inflammation‐related transcription factors in atherosclerosis (AS). We aimed to identify studies exploring aspirin's modulatory effects on transcription factors, specifically NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α, and their roles in AS‐related inflammatory processes. A comprehensive literature search was performed using PubMed, ScienceDirect, and Google Scholar, covering publications from 1980 to October 2025. Search terms included “Atherosclerosis,” “Aspirin,” “NF‐κB,” “PPARs,” “AP‐2α,” “Inflammation,” and “Transcription Factors.” Reference lists of included studies were manually reviewed to identify additional relevant articles. This search strategy yielded 102 studies for initial screening. Although the search covered a broad timeframe and multiple databases, the initial yield of 102 studies reflects the specificity of our inclusion criteria, which targeted studies directly addressing aspirin′s modulatory effects on the transcription factors NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α in atherosclerosis. Duplicate and irrelevant records were excluded to ensure that all eligible and high‐quality studies were captured within the defined scope.

sec.title: Eligibility Criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they investigated aspirin's effects on inflammation‐related transcription factors (NF‐κB, PPARs, AP‐2α) in the context of atherosclerosis, including preclinical (e.g., in vitro, animal models) or clinical studies. Eligible studies had to report outcomes related to inflammatory pathways, plaque stability, or cardiovascular complications in AS. Only English‐language articles published between 1980 and 2025 were considered to ensure accessibility and relevance. Exclusion criteria included studies not specifically addressing aspirin's effects on the specified transcription factors, research focused on non‐AS conditions (e.g., cancer), and nonoriginal data sources such as reviews, editorials, or conference abstracts. These criteria ensured that selected studies provided high‐quality insights into aspirin's anti‐inflammatory mechanisms and their therapeutic implications in AS management.

sec.title: Data Extraction
sec.p: To ensure a rigorous and unbiased review process, two independent reviewers screened titles and abstracts of identified studies against the inclusion and exclusion criteria. Full‐text articles of potentially relevant studies were then evaluated for eligibility by the same reviewers. Discrepancies between reviewers were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer to reach consensus. Data extracted included study design, transcription factors investigated, aspirin's mechanistic effects, and outcomes related to inflammation or AS progression. This systematic approach minimized selection bias and ensured the inclusion of high‐quality studies contributing to a comprehensive understanding of aspirin's role in modulating transcription factors in atherosclerosis.

sec.title: Study Selection
sec.p: Two independent reviewers (S. S. Bahreiny and M. Aghaei) assessed the eligibility of the retrieved records according to the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Initially, titles and abstracts were screened for relevance. Subsequently, the full texts of potentially eligible studies were retrieved and evaluated in detail by both reviewers. Any disagreements during the screening or eligibility assessment were resolved through discussion or by consulting a third reviewer (N. Saki). The entire study selection process, including the number of records identified, screened, excluded (with reasons), and finally included, is illustrated in Figure 1 (PRISMA 2020 flow diagram).

caption.p: Flow diagram of study selection adjusted by PRISMA.

sec.title: Nuclear Factor‐Kappa B (NF‐κB)
sec.p: The NF‐κB family includes five members, such as p50 and p65, which control inflammation by turning on specific genes. Normally, NF‐κB is inactive, held in the cell's cytoplasm by IκB proteins [14]. When inflammation signals, like TNF‐α or interleukins, are present, IκB is broken down, freeing NF‐κB to move into the nucleus. There, it activates genes that produce inflammatory molecules, adhesion proteins, and clotting factors, driving atherosclerosis progression. In the alternative pathway, NIK and IKKα mediate NF‐κB activation, which ultimately triggers the activation of adaptive humoral immunity [15].

AS is characterized by an imbalance between inflammatory and anti‐inflammatory responses [16]. NF‐κB is a widely expressed transcription factor in cellular responses to cell inflammatory signals and stimuli such as stress, cytokines, free radicals, irradiation, oxidized LDL (oxLDL), and infection antigens. In turn, NF‐κB stimulates cytokine production in a positive loop. The pathogenic role of NF‐κB has been demonstrated in many diseases, including cancer and CVD, by driving the expression of specific target genes involved in inflammation. Therefore, NF‐κB may be a promising therapeutic target in diseases that inflammation contributes to organ damage followed by increased activity of this transcription factor [7, 17, 18].

Several signaling pathways are involved in activation and inhibition of NF‐κB. For example, cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), a ubiquitous second messenger, inhibits NF‐κB in endothelial cells mediated by activation of protein kinase A (PKA). In contrast, mitogen‐activated protein kinase (P38/MAPK) phosphorylates and activates NF‐κB subunits in endothelial cells. Interestingly, cAMP inhibits P38/MAPK signaling as well [19].

Atherosclerosis is a multistage process that includes the following stages: damage to the endothelium, formation of foam cells, the proliferation of smooth muscle cells, and formation and rupture of the atherosclerotic plaque [20]. When endothelial cells (ECs) lose their ability to maintain homeostasis, vessel walls are vulnerable to vasoconstriction, lipid infiltration, leukocyte adhesion, platelet activation, and oxidative stress (Figure 2) [21].

caption.p: Schematic representation of aspirin's anti‐inflammatory mechanisms in atherosclerosis through modulation of transcription factors NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α. The figure summarizes key molecular interactions verified from the reviewed literature to illustrate aspirin's integrated role in endothelial protection and plaque stabilization.

sec.title: Endothelial Cell Dysfunction, Inflammation, and Atherosclerosis
sec.p: Mediated by NF‐κB activation, trapped modified LDL induces the production of ROS and promotes the synthesis of pro‐atherogenic cytokines (TNF‐α and interleukins IL‐1 and IL‐6), adhesion molecules (VCAM‐I and ICAM‐I), chemokines (MCP‐1 and IL‐8), abnormal vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation (VSMC), and prothrombotic mediators such as plasminogen activator inhibitor (PAI) or tissue factor [20, 22, 23]. In a positive loop, IL‐1β induces nuclear transfer of NF‐κB through activation of the p38/MAPK pathway [23, 24, 25].

Hyperglycemia and ox‐LDL stimulate endothelial cells activate protein kinase C (PKC). PKC, through NADPH activation, generates ROS agents that subsequently activate NF‐κB. NADPH oxidase 4 (NOX4) is an essential factor for the production of ROS [26]. Increased expression of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in pathological states also leads to high production of NO, which generates nitrite peroxides in its reaction with produced ROS agents and causes vascular damage [27]. Expression of both NOX4 and iNOS increases at AS (Figure 2) [28].

Moreover, the renin–angiotensin system, particularly angiotensin II (Ang II) as a strong vasoconstrictor, is produced during hypertension. In fact, Ang‐II possesses the effects of inflammation, pro‐atherogenic, and induces endothelial oxidative stress [22]. Ang II activates the P38/MAPK/NF‐κB signaling pathways [29] and NADPH oxidase [7]. Moreover, in the response to inflammation in VSMC cells, Ang II by binding to the angiotensin II type 1 receptor (AT1R) promotes the expression of COX‐2. Subsequently, COX‐2 activates NF‐κB and secretion of C‐reactive protein (CRP) [30]. CRP is a pro‐atherogenic factor that activates VSMC, upregulates the expression of AT1R, various adhesion molecules, and PAI‐1, but it decreases NO levels [7].

sec.title: The Role of Nitric Oxide (NO) in Endothelial Dysfunction
sec.p: NO is synthesized in ECs from l‐arginine in a reaction catalyzed by endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) [20, 31]. NO plays anti‐atherogenic role, therefore, a decrease in the NO level could promote the progression of atherosclerosis and CVD [21]. By increasing the expression of IκB and binding to a critical cysteine in the NF‐κB subunit P50, NO inhibits NF‐κB [32]. In situations of oxidative stress, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, smoking, or diabetes, NO is reduced [20, 33]. Moreover, AgII produces ROS elements and causes inactivation of NO during inflammation [32].

sec.title: LDL Infiltration and Foam Cell Formation of Monocytes and VSMCs
sec.p: Trapped LDL particles are oxidized in the form of ox‐LDL in the subendothelial space, producing a pro‐inflammatory response in ECs and macrophages [20].

Hydrolysis of packed cholesterol esters produces free cholesterol that is transferred by macrophages to apolipoprotein A‐I (ApoA‐I) or high‐density lipoprotein (HDL) (an essential step for removing excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues). The regular reverse cholesterol transport (RCT) process and HDL biogenesis are mediated by the ATP‐binding cassettes‐A1 (ABCA1) and ATP‐binding cassette transporter‐G1 (ABCG1), and scavenger receptor class‐B1 (SR‐B1). These cholesterol transporters are essential in mediating cholesterol efflux from cells and preventing foam cell formation [20, 22, 34].

However, the pro‐inflammatory microenvironment of atherosclerotic lesions impairs the ABCA1 efflux system in macrophages and promotes foam cell accumulation. Furthermore, excessive lipid uptake by macrophages enhances the inflammatory response, and oxLDLs induce signaling cascades that activate NF‐κB targets [20].

sec.title: Plaque Stability and Rupture
sec.p: When inflammation prevails and the death of VSMC happens, extracellular matrix (ECM) production is reduced, while the release of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) increases. MMPs degrade ECM components and promote the progression of AS. Consequently, the solid and rigid fibrous cap maintenance decreases, making the cap unstable and prone to cracking [20, 35]. Increased expression of MMP‐9 in AS is accompanied with degraded collagen type IV, a significant basement membrane component [36]. There is a binding site for NF‐κB located in the region of the MMP‐9 promoter [36].

Expression of membrane‐bound prostaglandin E synthase‐1 (mPGES‐1) and COX‐2 as inducible enzymes increases in response to inflammation in the shoulder region of the plaque [37]. MMP‐9 expression increases in response to NF‐κB and COX‐2/mPGES‐1 signaling pathway [38].

sec.title: Peroxisome Proliferator‐Activated Receptors (PPARs)
sec.p: PPARs are transcription factors that play critical roles in regulating of the gene expression that are responsible for energy metabolism, cellular development, differentiation, and inflammation [39, 40]. PPARα, PPARβ/δ, and PPARγ are three separate PPAR isoforms that each have unique tissue expression patterns, physiological roles, and ligand selectivity [10]. PPARs work with other receptors, such as RXR or LXR, to control genes that manage inflammation, cholesterol levels, and energy metabolism. When specific molecules bind to PPARs, they activate these receptors, allowing them to turn on genes that reduce inflammation or promote cholesterol removal. Without these molecules, PPARs remain inactive, blocking gene activation. PPARs also interact with other factors, like NF‐κB, to fine‐tune their effects on inflammation and atherosclerosis [41].

PPAR activation is essential for regulating sensitivity to inflammatory stimuli and controlling the production of proinflammatory and anti‐inflammatory factors [42, 43, 44]. PPARs were initially thought to have an anti‐inflammatory function. Through repression of inflammation‐associated NF‐κB, PPARδ‐ligands were found to suppress inflammation‐associated genes in human monocyte‐derived macrophages [41]. Activation of PPARα and PPARγ may lead to anti‐atherosclerotic effects by inhibiting the expression of proinflammatory cytokines in vascular cells (Figure 2) [42]. Under atherosclerotic conditions, the expression of these transcription factors decreases [38, 41, 42], which in turn leads to increased expression of MMPs [41, 42].

Therefore, the significance of PPARs‐targeting in medical research and medication discovery for hypertension or atherosclerosis (AS) is determined by PPARs' critical biological roles [40]. However, the effects of PPARs on inflammation are complex depending on the interactions with target gene sequences (PPREs) and the presence or absence of relevant PPAR ligands, coactivators, and repressors [41, 42].

Through triggering lipoprotein production, inflammatory response [40] and fatty acid and cholesterol metabolism, PPARα is primarily engaged in the regulation of the cell′s energy homeostasis [10]. By preventing leukocytes from adhering to activated endothelial cells in the arterial lumen during the AS process, PPARα activation prevents the development of macrophage‐derived foam cells and atherosclerotic plaques by reducing the oxidative modification of lipoproteins. In order to stop the inflammatory response, PPARα ligands can successfully block the NF‐кB activation and signaling pathways by lowering the amounts of IL‐1, TNF‐α, COX‐2, iNOS, and other proinflammatory cytokines. When PPAR is activated, CRP, IL‐6, and PGs levels are reduced [40]. Moreover, PPARα/γ can reduce MMP‐9 expression by inhibiting COX‐2/mPGES‐1 [45]. Studies in human monocyte THP‐1 cells have shown that using PPAR‐α or PPAR‐γ agonists reduces MMP‐9 secretion [46].

Membrane scavenger receptors perform ox‐LDL internalization into lysosomes to be hydrolyzed. Lysosomes and autophagy are involved in nearly the whole process of AS plaque formation and growth in foam cells [47]. The crucial regulator of the induction of autophagy and lysosome biogenesis is transcription factor EB (TFEB) [10]. Inhibition of TFEB is demonstrated in AS [47]. Notably, PPARα activation and recruitment to its binding site on the Tfeb promoter were improved by SR‐BI, which in turn controlled the expression of Tfeb. Additionally, TFEB controls PPARα expression. SR‐BI expression was also elevated by PPARα agonism. Contrarily, NF‐κB transcriptional activity may reduce the expression of the autophagy genes. Interestingly, by activation of PPARα, aspirin upregulates TFEB and consequently stimulates lysosomal biogenesis in brain cells [10].

By preventing NF‐kB activity, activated PPARγ encourages the outflow of cholesterol and lessens inflammation. RCT, which is mediated by ABCA1, ABCG1, PPARγ, and LXRα, is a process for the recycling or excretion of cholesterol in which cholesterol enters LDL, effluxes from foam cells, travels from blood arteries, and returns to the liver for processing. The PPARγ–LXRα–ABCA1/ABCG1 pathway has been implicated in numerous studies as playing a significant role and being a possible therapeutic target for atherosclerosis [22, 48]. Increased PPARγ expression suppresses NF‐κB, STAT1, and AP‐1 signal transduction to have anti‐inflammatory effects, regulates iNOS secretion, and decreases monocyte secretion of IL‐6, MCP‐1, TNF‐α, and other cytokines. The production of VCAM‐1 results in chronic inflammation, which is partially reduced by PPARγ [40].

BCL‐6 is released when PPARβ/δ is activated by a ligand, and it can block the pro‐inflammatory pathways that lead to the development of atherosclerosis [49]. Additionally, PPARβ/δ may lessen the circulatory system's ability to transmit signals associated with inflammatory responses [40].

sec.title: Activator Protein‐2α (AP‐2α)
sec.p: AP‐2α controls cell functions by targeting specific DNA regions in genes, influencing cell growth and differentiation. It is activated by enzymes like protein kinase D and AMPKα, which play roles in inflammation and atherosclerosis [1, 50].

AMPKα, a serine–threonine kinase, is activated by oxidants and hypoxia conditions [1, 51] and increases MMP‐2 expression in VSMCs [52], leading to instability of atherosclerotic plaques [53]. Additionally, several targeted genes, including ABCA1 and APOE, have been demonstrated to be repressed by AP‐2α phosphorylation [53]. Noteworthy, AP‐2α decreased HDL biogenesis by decreasing the expression of ABCA1 [50], thus may play a role in the progression of AS by increasing foam cell formation.

On the other hand, inhibition of oxidative stress by AP‐2α activation in human epithelial cells [1]. Phosphorylation and activation of AP‐2α by AMPKα can increase IκB expression and inhibit NF‐κB function (Figure 2) [1, 51]. Moreover, in diabetic mice ApoE−/−, hyperglycemia prevented AMPKα–AP‐2α signaling and decreased levels of the prolyl‐4‐hydroxylase alpha 1 (P4Hα1) that is an essential enzyme in type I and III collagen synthesis [53]. In Apoe−/− mice, activation of AP‐2α increased the stability of atherosclerotic plaque and Ang II‐induced aneurysm development. These disparities may be caused by the fact that the DNA sequences surrounding the binding site, among other things, influence whether AP‐2α interacts with the target genes and whether they are up‐ or downregulated [53].

PPARs and Ap‐2α transcription factors interact with other key transcription factors of the inflammatory pathway, such as NF‐κB, and play important roles in regulating inflammatory genes and modulating inflammation in AS [54]. Modulating the interaction between transcription factors and inflammatory networks could therefore be considered as important therapeutic targets for anti‐inflammatory mechanisms.

sec.title: Control of Inflammation by Aspirin: Implication for Transcription Factor
sec.p: Aspirin has displayed anti‐inflammatory properties [34]. It regulates the expression of inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and adhesion molecules by regulating the expression and function of related transcription factors [7, 28, 55]. For example, nucleotide‐binding oligomerization domain‐like receptor protein (NLRP‐1) inflammasome has been introduced as a main factor in the initiation and progression of the endothelial dysfunction that provokes atherosclerotic events [56]. Following aspirin intake by patients with peripheral arterial disease, lowering level of transcription of NLRP‐1 in endothelial cells (ECs) occurred. Noteworthy, this finding has been attributed to the possible inhibition of the NF‐κB pathway [57].

Aspirin's anti‐inflammatory effects in atherosclerosis are well‐documented, particularly through its modulation of transcription factors such as NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α. However, these effects are highly dose‐dependent, with low doses (e.g., 75–100 mg/day) typically associated with anti‐inflammatory and atheroprotective outcomes, while higher doses (e.g., > 1 g/day) may paradoxically promote pro‐inflammatory responses in certain contexts [58, 59, 60].

For example, high‐dose aspirin has been shown to enhance the production of pro‐inflammatory cytokines, such as IL‐6, in some in vitro models, potentially due to nonspecific inhibition of cyclooxygenase‐independent pathways [61, 62]. This dose‐dependent variability underscores the importance of optimizing aspirin dosing to maximize its anti‐inflammatory benefits while minimizing potential adverse effects in atherosclerosis management.

In this context, we investigated the modulatory effects of aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) on the inflammatory process of AS, focusing in particular on three important transcription factors such as NF‐κB, PPAR, and AP‐2α, as summarized in Table 1. To provide a clearer overview of the existing evidence, Table 2 summarizes the key findings from studies investigating aspirin's effects on major transcription factors involved in atherosclerosis, including NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α. The table outlines the type of study, the specific transcription factors assessed, and the primary outcomes related to inflammation, oxidative stress, and plaque stability. As shown, most available data support aspirin's inhibitory action on NF‐κB, leading to reduced inflammatory cytokine production and improved endothelial function. In contrast, studies on PPARs and AP‐2α are fewer but indicate potential anti‐inflammatory and plaque‐stabilizing roles through enhanced cholesterol efflux and reduced oxidative damage.

sec.p: Together, these findings reinforce aspirin's multifaceted mechanism of action in atherosclerosis and highlight important areas—particularly involving PPARs and AP‐2α—that require further mechanistic and translational investigation.

sec.title: Anti‐Inflammation Effect of Aspirin by NF‐κB
sec.p: Zhang and colleagues in an in vivo study showed that aspirin inhibited NF‐κB expression in mice by upregulating the cAMP and PKA, reducing the expression of adhesion molecules, chemokines, and inflammatory cytokines. These processes led to a decrease in cell proliferation of VSMCs, and ultimately inhibiting the progression of AS [55].

Some studies have also shown that aspirin inhibits the expression of chemokine (C‐X3‐C motif) ligand 1 (CX3CL1) (or fractalkine) by inhibiting the NF‐κB pathway [55]. Therefore, they showed the size reduction of atherosclerotic plaques in hyperlipidemic mice following the reduction of leukocyte adhesion [69, 74].

Yotsui and colleagues presented that aspirin inhibited NF‐κB signaling in IL‐1β‐activated aortic smooth muscle cells (AoSMCs), thereby reducing the expression of adhesion molecules. It caused a decrease in the adhesion of Jurkat T cells to AoSMCs. Thus, aspirin increased VSMC survival in the plaque by regulating the expression of adhesion molecules and ultimately strengthened the stability of the atherosclerotic plaque [24].

In the study of Yang and colleagues, aspirin also inhibited NF‐κB signaling in TNF‐α‐stimulated human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), and resulted in decreasing of the IL‐8 and MCP‐1 expression [67]. This declination, in turn, diminished the migration and adhesion of leukocytes to damaged endothelial cells [75], which negatively influenced the AS progression [67].

While aspirin has demonstrated significant anti‐inflammatory effects through NF‐κB inhibition in various preclinical and clinical models, its efficacy can vary across patient populations, particularly in those with comorbidities. For instance, studies have reported reduced responsiveness to aspirin′s anti‐inflammatory effects in patients with diabetes mellitus, potentially due to altered NF‐κB signaling pathways influenced by hyperglycemia or insulin resistance [33, 76, 77]. Similarly, in patients with chronic kidney disease, impaired endothelial function and heightened oxidative stress may diminish aspirin's ability to effectively suppress NF‐κB‐mediated inflammation [78, 79]. These findings suggest that the therapeutic benefits of aspirin in modulating inflammation may be context‐dependent, necessitating personalized approaches to its use in atherosclerosis management.

The neuroimmune guidance factor netrin‐1 is expressed as a chemoattractant factor by endothelial cells in two isoforms: full‐length (secreted isoform) and truncated (nuclear isoform). Full‐length is associated with the inhibition of monocyte and macrophage recruitment. Under pro‐atherogenic conditions, the balance of the two isoforms changes [80]. In an NF‐κB‐dependent pathway, transcription of the truncated form is higher than that of the full‐length. Aspirin inhibits the synthesis of the truncated form by inhibiting NF‐κB signaling. Aspirin also increases the expression of the full‐length (secreted) form through epigenetic changes and histone acetylation. Therefore, it reduces the accumulation of monocytes and macrophages in atherosclerotic plaques by establishing a balance between the two isoforms [70].

Aspirin plays an essential role in regulating inflammatory factors as an antioxidant and inhibitor of the NF‐κB pathway in AS. Studies have shown that treating human ox‐LDL‐stimulated umbilical vein cells with aspirin inhibits NF‐κB/p65 and subsequently reduced the expression of NOX4 and iNOS [28]. Aspirin treatment can reduce the expression of adhesion molecules (e.g., VCAM‐1 and ICAM‐1) in endothelial cells by inhibiting ROi production and subsequent NF‐κB inhibition [68]. Notably, the inhibition of NF‐κB‐dependent adhesion molecules by aspirin is most pronounced at low doses, as higher doses may disrupt this balance and promote inflammation in certain contexts [76, 81].

Aspirin has been reported to increase the expression of SR‐BI in human plaque through an NF‐κB‐dependent mechanism. No response element for NF‐kB was found in the gene sequence SR‐BI. NF‐κB may indirectly regulate the expression of SR‐BI at the posttranscriptional level through other factors [71].

Moreover, aspirin inhibits NF‐κB by inhibiting IκB phosphorylation and subsequently decreases the expression of COX‐2 in ox‐LDL‐stimulated HUVECs [63]. Furthermore, aspirin decreases MMP‐9 expression in TNF‐α‐stimulated RAW267.7 cells by inhibiting the MAPK/NF‐κB pathway and stabilizing atherosclerotic plaques [36].

Based on the aforementioned studies, aspirin has a protective effect against endothelial cell damage. Inhibition of NF‐κB also reduces the expression of adhesion molecules, chemoattractant chemokines, and other inflammatory factors. Aspirin also increases the expression of SR‐BI through the NF‐κB‐dependent mechanism, thereby decreasing foam cell content (Table 1). These processes result in decreased plaque formation, increased plaque stability, and inhibition of rupture and thrombosis, ultimately reducing the progression of AS.

sec.title: Anti‐Inflammation Effect of Aspirin by PPARs
sec.p: To point to the importance of PPARs in CVD management, it is noteworthy to mention that many conventional pharmacological therapies like Aspirin often affect PPARs [49]. Additionally, studies have shown that PPARα agonists can prevent AS plaque in coronary arteries from progressing [40].

Aspirin acts as a PPAR activator in modulating inflammation in AS [42]. AMPK may be regulated by PPARδ. In vivo and in vitro studies in the AS state also show that aspirin boosts PPAR‐δ and activates a pathway involving AMPK and PGC‐1, which increases ABCA1 to promote cholesterol removal and reduce inflammation. Whereas it decreases TNF‐α and NF‐κB expression by this signaling pathway. These events lead to increased vasodilation, diminished fat accumulation, and decreased inflammation [65].

Studies have also shown that low‐dose aspirin therapy increases the expression of ABCA1 and the efflux of ApoA‐I‐mediated cholesterol in RAW264.7 cells through a PPARα‐mediated mechanism. This process may reduce foam cell content and atherosclerosis lesions [66].

Exposure of human ox‐LDL‐stimulated macrophage cells to aspirin increased PPARα/γ expression, which subsequently decreased MMP‐9/2 expression and increased plaque stability by inhibiting NF‐κB [35]. Another study reported that exposure of macrophage‐derived THP‐1 cells to aspirin decreased MMP‐9 expression by two different mechanisms. The first mechanism involves inhibiting NF‐κB and subsequently reducing MMP‐2 expression through induction of high PPAR α/γ expression. In the second mechanism, aspirin inhibits prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) synthesis and decreases MMP‐9 expression by inhibiting COX‐2/mPGES‐1 [45].

Aspirin and PPARα activators such as fenofibrate and clofibrate inhibit NF‐κB and activator protein‐1 (AP‐1) by inhibiting PKC and reducing MCP‐1 expression [64]. MCP‐1 can promote MMP‐1 expression [64], hence aspirin could induce plaque stability by reducing MMP‐1/9 expression [82].

Through its binding to PPARα, aspirin stimulates the production of neurotrophin, which in turn has neuroprotective effects. One component of the cardiovascular effect of aspirin therapy mediated by PPARα may be neurotrophins with the role in the maintenance of cardiometabolic balance and cardioprotection. Interestingly, to note that COX inhibition, namely the antiangiogenic effects of aspirin, has also been linked to the antiangiogenic effects of PPARα activation. Aspirin dramatically decreased VEGF release in a limited clinical investigation with patients with myocardial ischemia having coronary artery bypass graft surgery; this antiangiogenic mechanism was also attributed to PPARα and γ activation [49].

Aspirin may boost PPARs expression on its own or in combination with PPAR activators, which reduces foam cell concentration and prevents plaque instability or rupture [83]. Therefore, it would be interesting to find out how much the antiangiogenic effects of aspirin are mediated by PPARs activation. Would be interesting to determine to what extent antiangiogenic effects of aspirin are mediated by PPAR activation [49].

sec.title: Anti‐Inflammation Effect of Aspirin by AP‐2α
sec.p: Several studies suggest that AP‐2α plays an essential role in regulating the expression of various factors involved in AS. Metformin increased P4Hα1 expression and, hence, collagen synthesis by activating AMPK‐AP‐2α signaling, subsequently increasing plaque stability in AS patients [53]. In vascular cells, aspirin stimulates AMPK. In VSMC, AMPK activation results in an increase in AP‐2α phosphorylation and inhibits the development of atherosclerotic plaques in mice. Aspirin also reduces inflammation through pathways independent of COX, involving the AP‐2α protein, which helps reduce cholesterol accumulation in plaques by activating AP‐2α and decreasing foam cell formation [1, 84].

Nonetheless, few studies have been performed on the role of aspirin in regulating Ap‐2α signaling in AS. Regarding the effect of aspirin on AP‐2α expression in AS, one study reported that using a conventional dose of aspirin increased AP‐2α phosphorylation and its activation. Subsequently, it inhibits NF‐κB by increasing the expression of IκBα, which ultimately inhibits oxidative stress and plaque instability in AS [1, 11].

It is important to note that, compared to the extensive evidence available for NF‐κB, studies directly investigating aspirin's regulatory effects on AP‐2α and PPARs in atherosclerosis remain relatively limited. The molecular pathways involving these transcription factors are emerging areas of research, and most current findings derive from small‐scale preclinical or in vitro studies. This limitation explains the more detailed discussion of NF‐κB in this review, while also emphasizing the need for future mechanistic and translational studies to better elucidate aspirin's effects on AP‐2α and PPAR‐mediated pathways.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Preclinical and clinical studies have demonstrated that inflammatory responses are involved in all stages of the atherosclerotic process, including onset, progression, and lesion formation. The inflammatory process is usually associated with multiple risk factors that cause changes in vascular pathophysiology, plaque formation, and thrombosis. Inflammatory cytokines play a crucial role in the rupture of vulnerable plaques through increasing accumulation of immune cells in the vessel wall, decreasing integrity of ECs following high level of surface adhesion molecules, low level of NO, and secretion of matrix‐degrading enzymes (e.g., MMPs) [21].

Dysregulation of the inflammatory transcription factor NF‐κB leads to various pathological events and chronic inflammatory disorders. Activation canonical (or classical) pathway of NF‐κB in response to inflammatory mediators leads to the transcription of genes such as inflammatory factors, adhesion molecules, and thrombosis factors. These genes play a central role in all phases of inflammation and atherosclerotic plaque formation [85].

Although aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is regarded to effect on AS through its antiplatelet property, its anti‐inflammatory effects are more associated with the amelioration of AS. In a dose‐dependent manner, as monotherapy or combination therapy, aspirin regulates critical molecules involved in inflammation process by influencing on the related transcription factors [64].

To prevent vascular wall damage, it is crucial to reduce cholesterol accumulation and/or the production of inflammatory mediators [40]. Numerous studies have shown that aspirin inhibits NF‐κB canonical signaling pathway, and modulates inflammation at AS [86]. By inhibiting NF‐κB, aspirin reduces the production of oxidants, but stimulates the production of NO [28], and inhibits the production of adhesion molecules and inflammatory cytokines, thereby inhibiting the formation of atherosclerotic plaques [55]. Moreover, the transcription factor NF‐κB also controls anti‐inflammatory genes such as the membrane protein SR‐BI. Therefore, studies on aspirin application in combination with the NF‐κB blocker are proposed to determine the anti‐inflammatory role of NF‐κB in macrophages in atherosclerotic plaques [87].

In contrast, the transcription factors PPARs and AP‐2a form a network in a negative reaction with NF‐κB, which attenuate the AS process. Aspirin increases the expression of membrane proteins and enhances HDL biogenesis that reduces foam cell content by increasing the expression of PPARs [66]. Aspirin also reduces the expression of MMPs through high expression of PPARs [88].

Therefore, aspirin could directly inhibit NF‐κB and inflammation in atherosclerosis, while it can increase the expression of PPARs and/or AP‐2 factors. In addition to its molecular effects, recent clinical evidence informs aspirin's role in atherosclerosis. Clinical trials like ASPREE and ARRIVE have shown varied results for aspirin's cardiovascular benefits beyond preventing blood clots, particularly in older adults or high‐risk groups. These differences may stem from variations in inflammation processes involving NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α [73, 89, 90, 91, 92]. These findings underscore the translational relevance of aspirin's effects on transcription factors discussed herein and highlight the need for further research to optimize its use in specific patient groups, particularly those with varying inflammatory profiles. Despite promising molecular evidence, large‐scale clinical trials such as ASPREE and ARRIVE have reported mixed results regarding aspirin's cardiovascular benefits. This variability may arise from several factors, including differences in study populations (e.g., age, baseline inflammation, diabetes, or CKD status), aspirin dose and regimen, and background therapies. Moreover, interindividual variability in the activation of transcriptional pathways such as NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α may further modulate aspirin's anti‐inflammatory and plaque‐stabilizing effects. These findings highlight that aspirin's efficacy is context‐dependent and suggest that personalized approaches—guided by molecular and clinical biomarkers—may be necessary to optimize its therapeutic use in atherosclerosis prevention and management [91, 93, 94, 95, 96, 97, 98]. To advance the therapeutic potential of aspirin in atherosclerosis, future research should focus on clarifying the role of AP‐2α in modulating inflammatory and plaque‐stabilizing pathways, given its emerging significance in aspirin's non‐COX‐dependent effects. Additionally, exploring aspirin in combination therapies, such as with statins or novel anti‐inflammatory agents, could enhance its efficacy in CVD by targeting complementary pathways like NF‐κB and PPARs. These investigations will help address current gaps and guide personalized treatment strategies for diverse patient populations [99, 100]. Nonetheless, the relationship between AP‐2a and PPARs transcription factors in atherosclerosis and the effect of aspirin on them need to be more investigated. While aspirin's modulation of NF‐κB, PPARs, and AP‐2α offers significant therapeutic potential, its efficacy is influenced by dose‐dependent effects and patient‐specific factors, such as comorbidities like diabetes or chronic kidney disease, which warrant further investigation to optimize its clinical application.
